scenario: Italian A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In Italian, how do you say 'I am'? {{c1::Io sono}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Io sono | Meaning: I am
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'I am' en Italian? Io sono

scenario: Italian A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In Italian, how do you say 'They are'? {{c1::Loro sono}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Loro sono | Meaning: They are
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'They are' en Italian? Loro sono

text: In Italian, the subject pronouns for 'I' and 'you (singular informal)' are {{c1::io}} and {{c2::tu}}.
explanation: Italian pronouns are often omitted in sentences because the verb conjugation clearly indicates the subject.
usage: Examples: Io sono stanco. (I am tired.) | Tu sei pronto? (Are you ready?)
spanish: En italiano, los pronombres de sujeto para 'yo' y 'tú' son 'io' y 'tu'.

text: In Italian, the subject pronouns for 'he' and 'she' are {{c1::lui}} and {{c2::lei}}.
explanation: In grammatical descriptions, you might see 'egli' (he) and 'ella' (she), but in modern spoken Italian, 'lui' and 'lei' are exclusively used.
usage: Examples: Lui è bravo. (He is good.) | Lei è italiana. (She is Italian.)
spanish: En italiano, los pronombres de sujeto para 'él' y 'ella' son 'lui' y 'lei'.

text: In Italian, the subject pronoun for 'we' is {{c1::noi}}, and for 'you all (plural)' is {{c2::voi}}.
explanation: Unlike Spanish, Italian 'noi' and 'voi' do not change ending for gender (no 'nosotras' or 'vosotras').
usage: Examples: Noi siamo amici. (We are friends.) | Voi siete pronti? (Are you all ready?)
spanish: En italiano, los pronombres para 'nosotros' y 'vosotros/ustedes' son 'noi' y 'voi' (invariables en género).

text: In Italian, the subject pronoun for 'they' is {{c1::loro}}.
explanation: Used for both masculine, feminine, or mixed groups.
usage: Example: Loro sono stanchi. (They are tired.)
spanish: En italiano, el pronombre para 'ellos/ellas' es 'loro'.

text: In Italian, what is the infinitive form of the verb 'to be'? {{c1::essere}}
explanation: Essere is one of the two auxiliary verbs in Italian.
usage: Term: essere | Example: essere o non essere (to be or not to be)
spanish: ¿Cuál es el infinitivo del verbo 'ser/estar' en italiano? essere.

text: Conjugate 'essere' (to be) in the present tense for 'io' (I) and 'tu' (you): io {{c1::sono}}, tu {{c2::sei}}.
explanation: Essere present tense conjugations are highly irregular.
usage: Examples: Io sono felice. (I am happy.) | Tu sei qui. (You are here.)
spanish: Conjuga 'essere' en presente para 'yo' y 'tú': io sono, tu sei.

text: Conjugate 'essere' (to be) in the present tense for 'lui/lei' (he/she) and 'noi' (we): lui/lei {{c1::è}}, noi {{c2::siamo}}.
explanation: Note the grave accent on 'è' (is). Without the accent, 'e' means 'and'.
usage: Examples: Lui è stanco. (He is tired.) | Noi siamo a casa. (We are at home.)
spanish: Conjuga 'essere' en presente para 'él/ella' y 'nosotros': lui/lei è, noi siamo.

text: Conjugate 'essere' (to be) in the present tense for 'voi' (you all) and 'loro' (they): voi {{c1::siete}}, loro {{c2::sono}}.
explanation: Notice that 'sono' is used for both 'io' (I am) and 'loro' (they are). Context tells them apart.
usage: Examples: Voi siete italiani? (Are you all Italian?) | Loro sono stanchi. (They are tired.)
spanish: Conjuga 'essere' en presente para 'vosotros' y 'ellos': voi siete, loro sono.

text: In Italian, the polite/formal pronoun for 'you (singular)' is {{c1::Lei}}.
explanation: It is grammatically feminine (same as 'she') and conjugates with the third-person singular verb form (e.g. 'Lei è' = You are formal), but refers to both men and women.
usage: Example: Signore, Lei è molto gentile. (Sir, you are very kind.)
spanish: En italiano, el pronombre formal/cortés para 'usted' es 'Lei' (con mayúscula). Usa la tercera persona singular del verbo.

text: To make a sentence negative in Italian, you place the word {{c1::non}} directly before the verb. Example: I am not Italian = {{c2::Non sono italiano}}.
explanation: Very simple negation structure, placed right before the verb.
usage: Example: Lui non è stanco. (He is not tired.)
spanish: Para hacer una oración negativa en italiano, se coloca 'non' justo antes del verbo. 'Non sono italiano' = No soy italiano.

text: To ask a question in Italian, you do not need to change the word order or use auxiliary verbs; you simply raise your {{c1::intonation}} at the end of the sentence.
explanation: Word order remains identical to statements. Just change the pitch.
usage: Statement: Sei stanco. (You are tired.) | Question: Sei stanco? (Are you tired?)
spanish: En italiano, no es necesario cambiar el orden de las palabras para hacer preguntas; solo se eleva la entonación al final.

text: In Italian, possessive adjectives usually require a definite article. For example, 'my book' is '{{c1::il mio libro}}' and 'my house' is '{{c2::la mia casa}}'.
explanation: mio/mia are masculine/feminine. Unlike Spanish, you must include 'il/la' in front.
usage: Examples: Questo è il mio libro. (This is my book.) | La mia macchina è rossa. (My car is red.)
spanish: En italiano, los adjetivos posesivos suelen requerir un artículo definido. 'il mio libro' = mi libro.

text: In Italian, 'this' is '{{c1::questo}}' (masc) / '{{c1::questa}}' (fem) and 'that' is '{{c2::quello}}' / '{{c2::quella}}'.
explanation: These agree in gender and number with the noun they modify.
usage: Examples: questo tavolo (this table) | quella sedia (that chair)
spanish: En italiano, 'este' se dice 'questo/questa' y 'ese/aquel' se dice 'quello/quella'.

text: In Italian, adjectives must agree in gender and number. If 'He is happy' is 'Lui è felice', 'They (masc) are happy' is '{{c1::Loro sono felici}}'.
explanation: Adjectives ending in '-e' become '-i' in the plural for both masculine and feminine.
usage: Example: Le ragazze sono felici. (The girls are happy.)
spanish: En italiano, los adjetivos concuerdan en género y número. 'Lui è felice' -> 'Loro sono felici' (las palabras terminadas en -e cambian a -i).
